Tired of Windows 10 Forcing Updates on You? Here's How to Disable Auto-Updates

I've got two Windows machines at home. Neither has moved to Windows 11 yet, and Windows' forced-update behavior is the worst — slow downs mid-work, random new bugs post-update. So this post is a note on how to turn off Windows 10's forced auto-updates.
Worth flagging up front — disabling Windows 10's forced updates means you also miss security patches, which is a real risk. If you're not sure what you're doing, leave the default alone.
(The voice in my head: "But Windows updates fail SO often!~~~~~~~")
Common reasons people want to kill this:
Updates also take forever. If you happen to need your computer right now and Windows decides to update — too bad.
Worst case is an update that errors out and gives you the dreaded "blue screen":
A blue screen is Windows' "something broke" page — white text on blue background with an error code. For most users it's just a wall of cryptic info.
Reminder again — if you turn off auto-updates, your machine has security risk exposure, and some software may stop working. If you're not sure what you're doing, leave it alone.
OK, if we're clear on the trade-off, here we go.
to open the Run dialog, type
key, you can also search "Run" in the start menu:
You'll land on the Services management window — that's the central panel for managing Windows services:
In the Services list, find "Windows Update" — that's the auto-update service. Press
You'll see its current state — "Running" + Startup type "Automatic". That's the forced-update behavior:
Right-click "Windows Update" → set "Startup type" to "Manual" or "Disabled", and set "Service status" to "Stop":
That's it — forced auto-updates are off.
Even with the service set to Manual / Disabled and Stopped, just opening the Windows Update settings page tends to re-start the service. So if you really want updates off, avoid opening that page.

Windows 10's forced update behavior is the worst — slowing your machine mid-work, throwing random new bugs, or worst of all bluescreen on a botched update. This post walks through disabling the Windows Update service from the Services UI. Heads up — this also disables security updates, so only do it if you understand the security trade-off.
